heroOverview: Orthopnea is breathlessness that occurs when lying flat and is promptly relieved by sitting or standing upright. It is a cardinal symptom of elevated left ventricular filling pressure — when a person lies down, venous return from the lower limbs increases by up to 30%, and a failing left ventricle cannot accommodate the surge, driving fluid into the pulmonary circulation. Clinicians quantify orthopnea by the number of pillows a patient needs to sleep comfortably (one-pillow, two-pillow, three-pillow orthopnea). Roughly 75% of patients with symptomatic heart failure experience orthopnea, making it one of the most specific physical examination questions for diagnosing decompensated cardiac failure. While heart failure is the dominant cause, bilateral diaphragm weakness, massive obesity, and large pleural effusions also produce posture-dependent breathlessness through overlapping mechanisms.

keyStats.prevalence: Reported by approximately 50-75% of patients hospitalised with decompensated heart failure; prevalence in the general population tracks with heart failure prevalence of roughly 2-3% of adults
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adults over 65 with left ventricular dysfunction, hypertension, or valvular heart disease; obesity-related orthopnea occurs at any adult age
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of presentation mirrors heart failure demographics: approximately 65-75 in high-income countries
keyStats.globalCases: Given the global heart failure burden of approximately 64 million people, orthopnea affects tens of millions of adults worldwide

definition: Orthopnea (ICD-10: R06.01) is dyspnoea that develops or worsens in the recumbent position and is relieved within minutes of sitting upright. The term derives from the Greek orthos (upright) and pnoia (breathing). The dominant mechanism is a postural redistribution of blood volume: in the supine position, blood pooled in the legs is returned to the central circulation, raising cardiac preload. A failing left ventricle cannot increase its stroke volume adequately (Starling failure), so left atrial and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure rises, driving fluid from capillaries into the pulmonary interstitium and alveoli. The resulting reduction in lung compliance and increased airway resistance causes breathlessness. Secondary mechanisms include upward displacement of the diaphragm by abdominal viscera when lying flat, which reduces functional residual capacity — this is why morbid obesity and ascites can also produce orthopnea. Orthopnea falls under general medicine for initial assessment and is classified under the specialty of cardiology when heart failure is the cause, pulmonology when respiratory muscle weakness or bilateral pleural disease is responsible, and critical care when it signals acute decompensation. It is an objective, reproducible symptom that strongly predicts elevated left ventricular filling pressures.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, caused by elevated pulmonary venous pressure from left ventricular failure, valvular heart disease, or constrictive pericarditis; it correlates with New York Heart Association functional class and worsens with decompensation.

Bilateral diaphragm weakness from neuromuscular disease (e.g. amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, bilateral phrenic nerve palsy) causes a disproportionate fall in tidal volume when supine because gravity no longer assists the accessory muscles; it is distinguished from cardiac orthopnea by the absence of crackles or elevated JVP.
Morbid obesity causes upward diaphragm displacement when lying flat and may coexist with obstructive sleep apnoea and obesity hypoventilation syndrome; it may improve substantially with weight loss.

A related phenomenon in which breathlessness occurs in only one lateral decubitus position (usually the affected side in unilateral pleural effusion or when the worse lung is dependent); it is mechanistically distinct from true orthopnea.

primarySymptoms: Breathlessness or a sensation of air hunger that develops within minutes of lying flat, the defining feature, typically relieved promptly by sitting upright or propping up on multiple pillows.
Nocturnal awakening with dyspnoea, often described as waking up gasping or feeling suffocated, prompting the patient to sit on the edge of the bed or stand up.
A need to sleep propped up on two or more pillows or in a recliner chair to maintain comfortable breathing throughout the night.
A sensation of chest heaviness or tightness in the recumbent position that resolves quickly when upright.
Peripheral ankle and leg oedema in the evenings, reflecting the same elevated venous pressure that drives orthopnea when the patient lies down.
Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea — sudden severe breathlessness that awakens from sleep, typically 1-2 hours after lying down, and is more dramatic than the positional breathlessness of mild orthopnea.
Fatigue and exertional breathlessness during the day, reflecting the underlying cardiac or respiratory limitation beyond the purely postural trigger.
Persistent dry cough when recumbent, caused by pulmonary oedema fluid irritating bronchial mucosa, occasionally the presenting complaint rather than frank dyspnoea.
Rapid shallow breathing in the supine position as the chest attempts to compensate for reduced respiratory reserve and increased pulmonary stiffness.
Abdominal fullness or bloating when lying flat, particularly when ascites or hepatomegaly accompanies right heart failure.
earlyWarningSigns: Adding an extra pillow to sleep comfortably, or moving from one to two pillows — even a one-pillow increase in a person with known heart failure can signal early decompensation.
Waking briefly in the night with mild breathlessness that settles quickly after sitting up, which may precede full paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea.
A gradual increase in ankle swelling over several days combined with reduced exercise tolerance.
A weight gain of more than 2 kg in 24-48 hours in a person with known heart failure, indicating fluid retention that predates symptomatic orthopnea.
emergencySigns: Severe breathlessness that does not resolve within a few minutes of sitting fully upright, suggesting acute pulmonary oedema requiring immediate emergency care.
Frothy, pink-tinged sputum, which is a sign of alveolar flooding and constitutes a medical emergency.
Cyanosis (blue lips or fingertips), a very fast heart rate (above 120 bpm), or low oxygen saturation on a home pulse oximeter (below 90%).
New chest pain accompanying orthopnea, raising the possibility of acute myocardial infarction precipitating acute left ventricular failure.
Confusion, cold clammy skin, or extremely low blood pressure alongside orthopnea, which may indicate cardiogenic shock.

causes.cause: Left ventricular systolic failure (reduced ejection fraction heart failure)
causes.description: Impaired left ventricular contraction from ischaemic cardiomyopathy, dilated cardiomyopathy, or myocarditis reduces stroke volume; the resulting increase in end-diastolic pressure is transmitted to the pulmonary veins, causing pulmonary congestion that worsens dramatically in the supine position.

causes.cause: Left ventricular diastolic failure (heart failure with preserved ejection fraction)
causes.description: Stiff, non-compliant ventricle walls in HFpEF — common in hypertension, diabetes, and older women — resist filling; venous return surges when lying flat and cannot be accommodated, raising filling pressures and causing pulmonary congestion without a reduced ejection fraction.

causes.cause: Valvular heart disease (mitral or aortic valve disease)
causes.description: Severe mitral regurgitation or mitral stenosis raises left atrial pressure; the supine position augments venous return and pushes the already-elevated pressure into the pulmonary vasculature, causing or worsening orthopnea even when ventricular function is preserved.

Bilateral phrenic nerve palsy (e.g. from motor neuron disease, Guillain-Barré syndrome, or bilateral cervical nerve root disease) reduces diaphragmatic contribution to breathing; this weakness is most apparent supine when gravitational assistance from abdominal content displacement is lost.

Excess abdominal adiposity or a large volume of ascitic fluid displaces the diaphragm cranially in the supine position, reducing lung volumes and tidal volume; functional residual capacity can fall by more than 50% from standing to supine in morbid obesity.

Fluid in both pleural spaces compresses adjacent lung tissue; lying flat increases the hydrostatic column of fluid against the lung, further reducing functional lung volume and worsening breathlessness.

Fibrotic pericardial constriction impairs ventricular filling; the increased venous return of the supine position overloads both ventricles simultaneously, causing venous congestion and breathlessness.

riskFactors.description: The single most important risk factor; orthopnea is present in approximately 75% of hospitalised heart failure patients and tracks with New York Heart Association class III-IV. Optimising heart failure therapy reduces orthopnea severity.

Chronic uncontrolled hypertension leads to left ventricular hypertrophy and diastolic dysfunction, the foundation of HFpEF; blood pressure control to guideline targets reduces the progression to heart failure and orthopnea.

Myocardial infarction damages ventricular muscle and is the leading cause of systolic heart failure; reducing cardiovascular risk with statins, antiplatelet therapy, and revascularisation limits the development of ischaemic cardiomyopathy.
Excess abdominal adiposity directly compresses the diaphragm supine; obesity also drives hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and obstructive sleep apnoea, which cumulatively increase heart failure risk.

Diabetic cardiomyopathy causes diastolic dysfunction independent of coronary disease; diabetics have a two- to fivefold higher risk of developing heart failure and thus orthopnea compared to non-diabetics.
Rheumatic or calcific valve disease elevates left atrial pressure chronically; decompensation produces orthopnea that resolves only with definitive valve repair or replacement.

Conditions causing bilateral diaphragm weakness — amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, myasthenia gravis, bilateral phrenic nerve injury — produce orthopnea through a respiratory rather than cardiac mechanism, and are managed with nocturnal non-invasive ventilation.

Obstructive sleep apnoea causes repetitive nocturnal hypoxia and sympathetic activation that accelerates left ventricular dysfunction; CPAP therapy reduces both sleep apnoea burden and cardiac filling pressures.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 65 with established heart failure or hypertensive cardiomyopathy
Patients with valvular heart disease, particularly rheumatic mitral stenosis in developing countries
Individuals with morbid obesity at any adult age
Patients with neuromuscular diseases affecting the diaphragm, including ALS and myasthenia gravis
Post-myocardial infarction patients with reduced left ventricular ejection fraction
Women over 70 with diabetes and hypertension (HFpEF demographic)
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of orthopnea begins at the bedside. The clinician quantifies severity by asking how many pillows the patient sleeps on and whether this has changed; a recent increase of even one pillow is clinically significant. Physical examination looks for elevated jugular venous pressure, a displaced cardiac apex, a third heart sound (S3), bibasal crackles, and dependent oedema — all signs of elevated cardiac filling pressures. Blood tests include B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) or N-terminal pro-BNP (NT-proBNP), which are elevated (typically above 400 pg/mL in acute decompensation) when heart failure is the cause, and near-normal in non-cardiac orthopnea. A chest X-ray can demonstrate cardiomegaly, pulmonary vascular redistribution, Kerley B lines, or pleural effusions. Transthoracic echocardiography is the key diagnostic investigation — it quantifies ejection fraction, diastolic function, valvular disease, and filling pressures non-invasively, guiding the selection of therapy. In suspected bilateral diaphragm weakness, sniff nasal inspiratory pressure or fluoroscopic sniff test assesses diaphragm function. Spirometry with supine versus upright vital capacity helps quantify the postural fall in lung function in neuromuscular cases; a fall of more than 20% from seated to supine is diagnostic of bilateral diaphragm weakness.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The cornerstone investigation: assesses left ventricular ejection fraction, diastolic function grade, valvular disease, and estimated pulmonary artery pressure to identify the cardiac cause of orthopnea.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound gel is applied to the chest and a probe moved over the skin; the test takes 20-40 minutes, involves no radiation, and does not require any preparation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Natriuretic peptides are released by stretched ventricular myocardium; elevated levels (BNP >100-400 pg/mL, NT-proBNP >300-900 pg/mL depending on cut-off) strongly support a cardiac cause and help gauge decompensation severity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm; results are usually available within hours in hospital settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects cardiomegaly, pulmonary venous congestion (upper lobe diversion), interstitial oedema (Kerley B lines), frank alveolar oedema, and pleural effusions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You stand in front of the X-ray machine, hold a deep breath briefly; the study takes under five minutes and delivers minimal radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A fall in forced vital capacity of more than 20% from the seated to the supine position points to bilateral diaphragm weakness rather than heart failure as the cause of orthopnea.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You breathe forcefully into a spirometer mouthpiece; the test is repeated lying flat, taking a further 10-15 minutes to complete.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies ischaemia, left ventricular hypertrophy, arrhythmia (especially atrial fibrillation), or bundle branch block that may underlie or precipitate cardiac failure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Adhesive electrodes are placed on the chest, arms, and legs; the recording takes about 5 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood electrolytes, renal function, full blood count, and thyroid function
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for precipitants of cardiac decompensation including anaemia, renal failure, electrolyte disturbances, and thyroid disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1-4 hours in most hospital laboratories.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is directed at the underlying cause. For cardiogenic orthopnea from heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), guideline-directed medical therapy forms the foundation: ACE inhibitors or ARBs (or ARNI — sacubitril/valsartan), beta-blockers, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists, and SGLT2 inhibitors have each independently shown to reduce mortality and hospitalisation. Diuretics, particularly loop diuretics such as furosemide, are the most rapidly effective agents for relieving acute orthopnea by reducing pulmonary venous congestion; intravenous furosemide in decompensated heart failure typically reduces filling pressures within 1-2 hours and produces symptomatic relief within hours. For heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) reduce heart failure hospitalisation by approximately 20-30% and improve dyspnoea scores. Valvular disease causing orthopnea should be referred for surgical or percutaneous repair once symptoms develop, as medical therapy alone provides limited long-term benefit. Bilateral diaphragm weakness is managed with nocturnal non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP), which supports tidal volume and reduces the work of breathing during sleep, typically eliminating nocturnal orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea. Obesity-related orthopnea improves with sustained weight loss and CPAP therapy for concurrent sleep apnoea. Patients should be counselled about daily weighing and to seek urgent review for any acute weight gain of more than 2 kg in 24 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, torasemide)
medicalTreatments.description: Inhibit sodium-potassium-chloride co-transport in the loop of Henle, producing rapid diuresis that reduces circulating volume and thereby lowers pulmonary capillary wedge pressure. Intravenous administration is used for acute decompensation; oral dosing maintains euvolaemia chronically.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Intravenous furosemide relieves acute pulmonary congestion and orthopnea in approximately 80-90% of decompensated heart failure patients within 24 hours; response is monitored by urine output and weight.

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors or ARBs (or sacubitril/valsartan)
medicalTreatments.description: Renin-angiotensin system blockade reduces cardiac afterload and blunts the neurohormonal activation that drives ventricular remodelling. Sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto) provides additional benefit via neprilysin inhibition, with a 20% relative risk reduction in cardiovascular death versus enalapril in HFrEF.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sacubitril/valsartan reduced the composite endpoint of death or heart failure hospitalisation by approximately 20% compared to enalapril in the PARADIGM-HF trial of over 8,000 patients; dyspnoea scores improved within 8 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Beta-blockers (carvedilol, bisoprolol, metoprolol succinate)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce heart rate, blunt catecholamine excess, and allow reverse ventricular remodelling over weeks to months; they must be introduced at very low doses when the patient is euvolaemic, not during acute congestion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Beta-blockers reduced all-cause mortality by approximately 34% in HFrEF across major trials (MERIT-HF, CIBIS-II, COPERNICUS) and improve New York Heart Association class and dyspnoea over 3-6 months.

medicalTreatments.name: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin)
medicalTreatments.description: Originally developed for diabetes, SGLT2 inhibitors reduce heart failure hospitalisations by approximately 25-30% in both HFrEF and HFpEF through osmotic diuresis, reduced preload, and metabolic cardioprotective mechanisms, and now feature in all major heart failure guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the EMPEROR-Reduced trial, empagliflozin reduced the primary endpoint of cardiovascular death or HF hospitalisation by approximately 25% versus placebo over 16 months in roughly 3,700 patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP/NIV)
medicalTreatments.description: Positive-pressure ventilation via face mask supports breathing during sleep in patients with bilateral diaphragm weakness or obesity hypoventilation syndrome; it eliminates nocturnal orthopnea by maintaining tidal volume and relieving respiratory muscle fatigue.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NIV improves nocturnal oxygen saturation, reduces morning headache, and improves daytime function in approximately 70-80% of patients with neuromuscular-cause orthopnea within 2-4 weeks of initiation.

medicalTreatments.name: Spironolactone or eplerenone (mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce aldosterone-driven sodium retention and attenuate adverse cardiac remodelling; indicated in HFrEF with ejection fraction below 35% and in HFpEF in selected patients. Require monitoring of potassium and renal function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the EMPHASIS-HF trial, eplerenone reduced the composite of cardiovascular death or HF hospitalisation by approximately 37% versus placebo in HFrEF with EF below 35%.

surgicalOptions.name: Valve repair or replacement
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical or transcatheter repair of mitral or aortic valvular disease relieves the elevated left atrial and pulmonary venous pressure driving orthopnea; timing is guided by symptom severity, ventricular size, and function.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical mitral valve repair for degenerative mitral regurgitation has operative mortality below 1% in experienced centres, with over 95% of patients experiencing resolution or marked improvement in heart failure symptoms at one year.

surgicalOptions.name: Cardiac resynchronisation therapy (CRT) implantation
surgicalOptions.description: A specialised pacemaker that coordinates left and right ventricular contraction in patients with HFrEF and left bundle branch block; it improves ejection fraction, exercise capacity, and reduces filling pressures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: CRT-D reduces the composite of death and hospitalisation by approximately 35% in eligible patients and produces a measurable improvement in NYHA class in roughly 60-70% of those who respond.

surgicalOptions.name: Left ventricular assist device (LVAD) implantation
surgicalOptions.description: A mechanical pump supporting left ventricular output in advanced HFrEF; used as a bridge to transplantation or as destination therapy. Relieves symptoms including orthopnea in patients who have exhausted medical and conventional device therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Continuous-flow LVADs provide a 2-year survival of approximately 55-70% in advanced HFrEF, with significant improvement in NYHA class and quality of life in the majority of recipients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Elevation of the head of the bed
alternativeTreatments.description: A practical, immediate, non-pharmacological measure: raising the head of the bed by 15-30 cm reduces orthopnea severity by keeping the upper body partly upright, lowering pulmonary venous pressure relative to full supine positioning.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sodium and fluid restriction
alternativeTreatments.description: Limiting sodium intake to less than 2 g per day and total fluid intake to 1.5-2 litres per day reduces circulating volume in heart failure, complementing diuretic therapy and reducing the frequency of decompensation episodes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cardiac rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured, supervised exercise training for stable heart failure patients improves peak oxygen uptake by approximately 17% (meta-analysis), reduces hospitalisation, and meaningfully improves exercise tolerance and breathlessness during daily activities, complementing pharmacotherapy.

whySeeSpecialist: Orthopnea always represents a symptom with a significant underlying cause and should not be managed without a definitive diagnosis. Cardiology referral is indicated when echocardiography shows reduced ejection fraction, significant valvular disease, or elevated filling pressures, as guideline-directed therapy substantially reduces mortality. Pulmonology should evaluate cases where diaphragm weakness or bilateral lung disease is suspected. Sleep medicine is relevant when orthopnea overlaps with obstructive sleep apnoea or obesity hypoventilation.
doctorSelectionGuide: A cardiologist with access to echocardiography and heart failure management is the appropriate specialist for most cases of orthopnea. Look for a heart failure service with a multidisciplinary team including specialist nurses, dietitians for sodium restriction guidance, and access to device therapies (CRT, LVAD). If neuromuscular disease is suspected, a neurologist and respiratory physician with NIV expertise should be involved jointly.
preventionStrategies: Control hypertension and diabetes rigorously, as these are the two most modifiable precursors of the heart failure that causes most orthopnea.
Treat and monitor known coronary artery disease with optimal medical therapy to prevent progression to ischaemic cardiomyopathy.
Adhere to daily weights when prescribed for heart failure; act on a 2 kg weight gain within 24-48 hours by adjusting diuretics as directed.
Achieve and maintain a healthy body weight: losing 10% of body weight in obese patients can meaningfully improve functional capacity and reduce orthopnea severity.
Treat obstructive sleep apnoea with CPAP, which reduces the nocturnal haemodynamic stress that accelerates left ventricular dysfunction.
Avoid excessive fluid and sodium intake, including hidden dietary sodium in processed foods.
lifestyleModifications: Sleep with the head of the bed elevated by 15-30 cm using a wedge pillow or by raising the bed legs, which reduces preload and alleviates overnight orthopnea.
Weigh yourself each morning before eating, after urinating, and report a gain of more than 2 kg in 24-48 hours to your care team.
Distribute fluid intake through the day and avoid large drinks in the evening to reduce nocturnal filling pressure surges.
Limit sodium to under 2 g per day; read food labels, as processed foods contribute 70-80% of dietary sodium for most people.
Plan activities that require exertion for morning when lung function is optimal and fluid accumulation is at its lowest.
Wear compression stockings during the day to limit venous pooling in the legs and reduce the fluid shift that occurs at bedtime.

recommended: Low-sodium foods (fresh vegetables, fruits, unprocessed proteins) to limit fluid retention
A Mediterranean-style diet rich in olive oil, fish, and vegetables, which has evidence for reducing cardiovascular mortality
High-fibre foods to maintain bowel regularity, avoiding straining that increases cardiac preload
Small, evenly distributed meals throughout the day rather than large evening meals
avoid: High-sodium processed foods (canned soups, deli meats, fast food) which exacerbate fluid retention
Excess fluid in the evening, particularly in patients on diuretics who already reach daily limits
Alcohol, which is directly cardiotoxic, worsens cardiomyopathy, and impairs sleep quality
Liquorice-containing products, which cause sodium retention by mimicking aldosterone

exerciseGuidelines: Regular, structured exercise is recommended for stable heart failure patients — the HF-ACTION trial showed a 15% reduction in all-cause mortality or hospitalisation with supervised aerobic training. Aim for 30 minutes of moderate walking or cycling five times per week once stable. Avoid vigorous exercise during decompensation or immediately after a hospitalisation. Resistance training may complement aerobic exercise but should be supervised initially. Always stop if breathlessness, palpitations, chest pain, or dizziness develop during activity.
dailyManagement: Weigh daily in the morning and record results; share a weekly log with your care team.
Take all heart failure medications at the same time each day, even when feeling well, as missed doses commonly precipitate decompensation.
Keep a blood pressure and heart rate diary if prescribed, and attend monitoring appointments for potassium and renal function checks.
Prepare a symptom action plan with your heart failure nurse specifying exactly when to increase diuretics or seek urgent care.
Arrange an annual flu vaccination and pneumococcal vaccination, as respiratory infections are a leading precipitant of heart failure decompensation.
prognosis: Orthopnea itself resolves or substantially improves in most patients when the underlying cause is treated. In heart failure, achieving euvolaemia with diuretics typically resolves acute orthopnea within 24-72 hours. Long-term prognosis depends on the underlying diagnosis: HFrEF carries a 5-year mortality of approximately 50% without optimal therapy, but guideline-directed medical therapy with the four-pillar regimen (ACE inhibitor/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2 inhibitor) substantially improves survival, ejection fraction, and symptom burden. Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction has a similar or somewhat better prognosis in terms of survival but equivalent symptom burden. Valvular heart disease treated with timely repair carries excellent surgical outcomes with greater than 95% freedom from severe symptoms at one year. Orthopnea from neuromuscular diaphragm weakness is chronic and progressive, but NIV prevents respiratory failure and significantly extends quality and duration of life.
recoveryTimeline: Acute orthopnea from decompensated heart failure typically resolves within 24-72 hours of intravenous diuresis. Patients started on heart failure medications see symptomatic improvement over weeks to months as neurohormonal blockade reverses remodelling; ejection fraction improvement can take 3-6 months. Orthopnea from corrected valvular disease resolves within days of intervention. Obesity-related orthopnea improves gradually over weeks to months with weight loss. Neuromuscular orthopnea does not resolve but becomes manageable with NIV started promptly.
complications: Acute pulmonary oedema — severe, sudden flooding of alveoli that can be life-threatening if not treated within minutes with oxygen, diuretics, and vasodilators.

Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea — extreme breathlessness waking the patient from sleep and persisting 15-30 minutes even after sitting up, representing more severe cardiac decompensation than simple orthopnea.
Respiratory failure requiring intubation when acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema does not respond promptly to non-invasive management.
Sleep deprivation and impaired daytime function from chronic nocturnal orthopnea, contributing to falls, cognitive decline, and depression in older adults.
Progressive cardiac remodelling and worsening heart failure when orthopnea signals inadequate treatment of elevated filling pressures over months.

Cardiogenic shock — a life-threatening collapse in cardiac output that may accompany extreme decompensation and requires intensive care management including inotropes or mechanical circulatory support.

supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient resources on heart failure symptoms, treatment, and living with the condition from the American Heart Association.

supportResources.name: NHS — Heart failure
supportResources.description: UK patient guidance covering diagnosis, treatment, and self-management of heart failure including symptom management.

supportResources.name: Heart Failure Society of America — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Evidence-based self-care tools and symptom management guides for patients with heart failure from the Heart Failure Society of America.

sources.title: McDonagh TA et al. 2021 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of acute and chronic heart failure. Eur Heart J 2021
McMurray JJV et al. Angiotensin-Neprilysin Inhibition versus Enalapril in Heart Failure (PARADIGM-HF). NEJM 2014
Packer M et al. Cardiovascular and Renal Outcomes with Empagliflozin in Heart Failure (EMPEROR-Reduced). NEJM 2020
Zannad F et al. Eplerenone in Patients with Systolic Heart Failure and Mild Symptoms (EMPHASIS-HF). NEJM 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnoea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sleep apnoea causes nocturnal awakening with choking or gasping, but the breathlessness does not resolve by sitting up — it resolves when the obstructed upper airway reopens. Orthopnea resolves predictably within minutes of becoming upright, whereas sleep apnoea symptoms correlate with snoring pauses rather than posture.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anxiety disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anxiety can cause dyspnoea in any position and is provoked by psychological triggers rather than recumbency. Normal BNP, echocardiography, and a positional pattern that does not reliably worsen only when supine help distinguish anxiety from cardiac orthopnea.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dyspnoea (unspecified)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: General dyspnoea on exertion is not positionally triggered and occurs in many conditions; orthopnea is specifically supine-triggered breathlessness that implies elevated pulmonary venous pressure or diaphragm weakness and carries a more specific diagnostic implication.

confusedWithConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnoea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OSA causes snoring, witnessed apnoeas, and morning headaches; it may coexist with heart failure but its breathlessness is position-independent and is resolved by CPAP, not by sitting upright during an episode.

relatedConditions.name: Heart failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Heart failure is the dominant cause of orthopnea; virtually all patients with NYHA class III-IV heart failure experience orthopnea, and its severity tracks with the degree of cardiac decompensation.

relatedConditions.name: Heart failure (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: Unspecified heart failure encompasses HFrEF and HFpEF as causes of elevated filling pressure and positional breathlessness.

relatedConditions.name: Pleural effusion
relatedConditions.relevance: Bilateral pleural effusions from any cause — cardiac, malignant, or inflammatory — worsen breathlessness when supine as hydrostatic pressure increases against lung tissue.

relatedConditions.name: Pulmonary fibrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Reduced lung compliance in pulmonary fibrosis limits reserve; patients often become more breathless when lying flat as diaphragm excursion is mechanically restricted by the stiff lung.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe anaemia reduces oxygen delivery and increases cardiac output demands, precipitating or worsening heart failure symptoms including orthopnea in patients with borderline cardiac reserve.

faqs.question: What is orthopnea?
faqs.answer: Orthopnea is breathlessness that develops when you lie flat and is relieved within minutes of sitting or standing up. It is a cardinal symptom of elevated pressure in the lungs' blood vessels, most often caused by heart failure, and its severity is measured by how many pillows you need to breathe comfortably.

faqs.question: Why does lying down cause breathlessness in heart failure?
faqs.answer: When you lie down, blood that was pooled in your legs returns to the heart. A weakened heart cannot pump this extra volume forward, so pressure builds in the pulmonary veins and fluid leaks into the lungs. Sitting up shifts blood back to the legs and lowers lung pressure, relieving the breathlessness within minutes.

faqs.question: How many pillows is normal?
faqs.answer: Most people sleep comfortably with one pillow. Needing two or more pillows to avoid breathlessness when lying flat is clinically abnormal and should be discussed with a doctor. In heart failure, needing 3 or more pillows — or sleeping in a chair — indicates significant congestion.

faqs.question: Is orthopnea always caused by heart failure?
faqs.answer: No, though heart failure is the most common cause. Other causes include bilateral diaphragm weakness from neuromuscular disease, morbid obesity displacing the diaphragm when supine, large bilateral pleural effusions, and ascites. The distinction matters because treatment differs substantially between these causes.

faqs.question: What is the difference between orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea?
faqs.answer: Orthopnea is breathlessness that comes on predictably within minutes of lying flat and eases quickly with sitting up. Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea (PND) is more dramatic: the patient wakes from deep sleep 1-2 hours after lying down with severe breathlessness that takes 15-30 minutes and sometimes moving to a window to resolve. PND represents a more severe degree of cardiac congestion.

faqs.question: What tests confirm the cause of orthopnea?
faqs.answer: An echocardiogram is the most informative test — it assesses heart function, valve disease, and filling pressures. A BNP or NT-proBNP blood level distinguishes cardiac from non-cardiac causes; a chest X-ray identifies pulmonary oedema or pleural effusions. Spirometry done both seated and lying flat can reveal diaphragm weakness.

faqs.question: Can orthopnea be treated without medication?
faqs.answer: Non-pharmacological measures help but usually cannot replace medication in cardiac orthopnea. Elevating the head of the bed by 15-30 cm provides partial relief. Sodium restriction and limiting evening fluid intake reduce the magnitude of overnight fluid shifts. For mild cases, these measures supplement rather than replace diuretics.

faqs.question: How quickly does furosemide relieve orthopnea?
faqs.answer: Intravenous furosemide typically begins producing urine within 30-60 minutes and relieves pulmonary congestion within 1-4 hours in most patients with decompensated heart failure. The sensation of orthopnea usually eases within the first day of adequate diuresis. Oral furosemide acts more slowly, over 1-2 hours.

faqs.question: When is orthopnea an emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care immediately if breathlessness in the supine position is severe and does not ease within a few minutes of sitting fully upright, if you produce frothy pink sputum, if your lips or fingertips turn blue, or if you develop chest pain, severe sweating, or confusion. These signs indicate acute pulmonary oedema or cardiogenic shock.

faqs.question: Can obesity cause orthopnea?
faqs.answer: Yes. In morbid obesity (BMI above 40), abdominal fat compresses the diaphragm upward when lying flat, reducing lung volume and causing breathlessness. This is separate from cardiac orthopnea. Sustained weight loss of 10% or more significantly reduces orthopnea severity in obese patients without heart disease.

faqs.question: Does orthopnea go away with treatment?
faqs.answer: In most patients with heart failure, optimising diuretics and heart failure medications resolves orthopnea within days to weeks. In neuromuscular disease, orthopnea does not fully resolve but is well managed with non-invasive ventilation during sleep. Post-valve surgery orthopnea typically resolves rapidly as pulmonary pressures normalise.

faqs.question: What lifestyle change helps most with orthopnea?
faqs.answer: Elevating the head of the bed, restricting sodium to under 2 g per day, and weighing daily are the most impactful lifestyle changes for cardiac orthopnea. For those with obesity-driven orthopnea, weight loss has the greatest effect. Compression stockings during the day also limit overnight venous return surges.

faqs.question: Can orthopnea be caused by lung disease?
faqs.answer: Lung diseases rarely cause true orthopnea. The exception is bilateral diaphragm weakness, which can result from neuromuscular disease, and severe bilateral pleural effusions. COPD patients typically find breathing easier when leaning forward (tripod position), not when simply sitting up — a different positional pattern from true orthopnea.

faqs.question: Why do doctors ask about how many pillows you need?
faqs.answer: Pillow count is a quick, validated surrogate for orthopnea severity. Each additional pillow represents a step up in cardiac filling pressure. An increase in pillow requirement from one to two or three over days is one of the earliest signs of heart failure decompensation and should prompt an urgent clinical review and possible diuretic adjustment.

faqs.question: Is exercise safe with orthopnea?
faqs.answer: When the underlying heart failure is stable and the patient is euvolaemic (no active fluid retention), supervised cardiac rehabilitation exercise is safe, beneficial, and recommended by guidelines. Exercise should be stopped and medical advice sought if orthopnea suddenly worsens, or if new breathlessness, chest pain, or palpitations develop during activity.

faqs.question: What is the difference between orthopnea and trepopnea?
faqs.answer: Orthopnea is breathlessness specifically when lying flat, regardless of which side. Trepopnea is breathlessness that occurs only in one lateral position — usually lying on one particular side — and is caused by asymmetric disease such as a unilateral pleural effusion or lung lesion that becomes compressed when it is the dependent side.

faqs.question: Can SGLT2 inhibitors help orthopnea?
faqs.answer: Yes. SGLT2 inhibitors like empagliflozin and dapagliflozin are now first-line in heart failure guidelines. They reduce heart failure hospitalisations by 25-30% in both HFrEF and HFpEF and improve dyspnoea scores within weeks. They work partly through osmotic diuresis, which lowers filling pressures and reduces orthopnea.

faqs.question: Should I be weighed daily if I have heart failure?
faqs.answer: Yes. Daily morning weighing before eating, after urinating, is a cornerstone of heart failure self-management. A weight gain of more than 2 kg in 24-48 hours almost always precedes symptomatic decompensation, including orthopnea, by 1-3 days. Acting promptly — adjusting diuretics or seeking medical review — can prevent a hospital admission.

faqs.question: Can valvular heart disease cause orthopnea?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severe mitral stenosis or mitral regurgitation raises left atrial pressure; when lying flat, increased venous return amplifies this pressure into the pulmonary veins, causing orthopnea even if the left ventricle is structurally normal. Valve repair or replacement is the definitive treatment, as medical therapy does not permanently correct the mechanical obstruction or regurgitation.

metaDescription: Orthopnea is breathlessness when lying flat, most often from heart failure. Learn its causes, pillow grading, diagnostic tests, and heart failure treatments.
